preamble: The voiced aspirated velar stop [gʱ] (breathy-voiced or murmured stop) exists as a separate phoneme in South Asian languages. Unlike voiceless aspirated stops in Germanic languages (which are allophones), voiced aspirated stops in Hindi, Sindhi, and related languages are independent phonemes contrasting minimally with their unaspirated counterparts. This represents phonemic contrast, not allophony.

examples.note: Hindi maintains a four-way voicing contrast for velars: /k kʰ g gʱ/. The voiced aspirate /gʱ/ is a distinct phoneme, not an allophone of /g/.
Sindhi preserves the full Indo-Aryan voiced aspirate series; /gʱ/ is phonemically contrastive with /g/.

sources: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
Masica, C. P. (1991). The Indo-Aryan Languages. Cambridge University Press.
